projects.name: api-gateway
projects.description: Edge routing, rate limiting, error envelope

decisions.title: Error responses include a structured `status` field
decisions.summary: Consumers read status from a dedicated structured field in the error body — never by parsing the human-readable error text.
decisions.context: Several services derive request status from the gateway’s error response. Historically that meant **parsing the human-readable message text**, because the numeric error code is pinned by a contract with legacy services and cannot be repurposed. As more machine consumers came to depend on it, that text-parsing turned into a growing liability.
decisions.consequences: Consumers must migrate to the `status` field. `billing` still parses the text and is tracked as an open cross-project signal. The message text stays for humans and logs but is no longer load-bearing — a wording change can no longer take down a downstream service, closing the failure mode behind the `payments-worker` outage.

alternatives.option: Keep parsing the error text (status quo)
alternatives.why_rejected: Brittle — a copy change silently broke payments-worker with 500s. The text was never a real contract.

alternatives.option: Encode status only in the HTTP / error code
alternatives.why_rejected: The code is pinned by the legacy contract and cannot change without breaking legacy consumers.

decisions.title: Success is signaled by the HTTP status code alone
decisions.summary: Callers determine success or failure purely from the HTTP status code returned by the gateway.
decisions.context: The first version of the gateway exposed only standard HTTP semantics. Callers were expected to branch on the status code — `2xx` success, `4xx`/`5xx` failure — and nothing else.
decisions.consequences: Too coarse: a `4xx` couldn’t separate a retryable validation hiccup from a permanent rejection, so consumers started reading the error **text** to recover the nuance. That workaround became the next decision. **Superseded.**

alternatives.option: Return a status field in the body from day one
alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected as over-engineering at the time — the codes seemed expressive enough.

decisions.title: Status is read from the error text, not the error code
decisions.summary: Consumers parse the human-readable error message text to determine request status.
decisions.context: The gateway needs to tell consumers success from failure. The error **code** is fixed by a contract with legacy services and can’t be repurposed to carry status, so a different signal was needed — quickly.
decisions.consequences: Worked until the error wording changed without warning and `payments-worker` began returning 500s in production. That fragility is exactly what the later structured-`status`-field decision was created to remove. **This decision is superseded.**

alternatives.option: Change the error code to encode status
alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — legacy consumers pinned the code in their contract; changing it would break them.

decisions.title: Developer code and infrastructure services deploy separately
decisions.summary: Application deploys and infrastructure rollouts (proxies, mesh, timeouts) run on independent pipelines.
decisions.context: Application code and infrastructure (proxies, service mesh, timeouts) shipped through a single pipeline. Their change cadence and blast radius are very different, and coupling them made failures hard to attribute.
decisions.consequences: Infra rollouts no longer ride along with application deploys, so an infra change can’t masquerade as an app regression. Teams must now coordinate infra rollouts explicitly — tracked as a cross-project signal toward `notifications` after **INC-3042**.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — an infra-only change silently broke a service and developers couldn’t locate the cause (INC-3042).

decisions.title: All team services authenticate via the shared local OIDC
decisions.summary: Every service validates requests against one shared local OIDC provider rather than rolling its own auth.
decisions.context: Services were beginning to implement their own authentication — inconsistent, hard to audit, and slow to onboard. A single shared identity path was needed across the group.
decisions.consequences: Auth is uniform and new services join cheaply, but the provider becomes a **single point of dependency** for all six services. Any change to it must be broadcast group-wide before shipping — tracked as a standing cross-project signal.

alternatives.option: Each service implements its own auth
alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — inconsistent, hard to audit, and slowed onboarding.

decisions.title: All inter-service calls route through the gateway, never direct
decisions.summary: East-west traffic goes through the gateway; direct service-to-service calls are not allowed.
decisions.context:
- A central place for auth, rate limiting, and observability.
- Avoids a mesh of undocumented direct dependencies.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — creates hidden coupling and bypasses rate limiting and tracing.

decisions.title: Retries use exponential backoff with full jitter, max 5 attempts
decisions.summary: Failed calls retry with exponentially increasing, jittered delays, capped at five attempts.
decisions.context:
- Full jitter avoids synchronized retry storms.
- A hard cap prevents unbounded load during outages.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — synchronized retries create a thundering herd.
Rejected — amplifies load during an outage.

decisions.title: OIDC access tokens expire in 15 minutes; refresh is client-side
decisions.summary: Access tokens are short-lived (15 min) and refreshed silently on the client.
decisions.context:
- A short token lifetime limits the blast radius of a leaked token.
- Client-side refresh keeps it invisible to users.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — a larger window for misuse if a token leaks.

decisions.title: Inbound webhooks are idempotent, deduped by event ID
decisions.summary: Webhook processing is safe to run twice; duplicates are dropped using the provider event ID.
decisions.context:
- Providers retry webhooks; processing the same event twice must be safe.
- Event IDs give a natural dedupe key.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — providers deliver at-least-once; duplicates are normal.

decisions.title: Money is stored as integer minor units, never floats
decisions.summary: All monetary amounts are stored as integer minor units (e.g. cents).
decisions.context:
- Floats introduce rounding errors in money math.
- Integer minor units are exact and portable.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — rounding errors are unacceptable in billing.

decisions.title: Feature flags are evaluated per request, not cached per process
decisions.summary: Flag values are read at request time so rollouts take effect without a redeploy.
decisions.context:
- Rollouts take effect immediately without a redeploy.
- Per-request evaluation supports targeting.

alternatives.option: Cache flags per process at startup
alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — changes wouldn’t take effect until a redeploy.

decisions.title: Schema migrations are forward-only in production
decisions.summary: Production migrations only roll forward; there are no down migrations.
decisions.context:
- Down migrations are rarely tested and risky in production.
- Rolling forward with a new migration is safer and auditable.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — down paths are seldom tested and dangerous under load.

decisions.title: Notification fan-out goes through the queue, never synchronous HTTP
decisions.summary: Producers publish to a queue; consumers pull — no synchronous HTTP fan-out.
decisions.context:
- Decouples producers from a variable number of consumers.
- Absorbs spikes and isolates slow consumers.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — couples latency to the slowest consumer and drops messages on spikes.

decisions.title: Rate limits are keyed by API token, not client IP
decisions.summary: The gateway rate-limits per API token so clients behind shared NATs aren’t throttled together.
decisions.context:
- Clients behind shared NATs share an IP and were unfairly throttled.
- The API token identifies the actual caller.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — shared NATs caused unrelated clients to throttle each other.

decisions.title: All timestamps are stored UTC ISO-8601; no local timezones
decisions.summary: Timestamps are stored as UTC ISO-8601 strings and converted to local time only at the edge.
decisions.context:
- A single canonical timezone removes ambiguity across services.
- Convert to local only at the edge / UI.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — ambiguous and error-prone across services and DST.

decisions.title: Service-to-service auth uses short-lived OIDC tokens, not static keys
decisions.summary: Services authenticate to each other with short-lived OIDC tokens instead of static API keys.
decisions.context:
- Short-lived tokens limit the damage from a leak.
- Reuses the shared OIDC provider instead of static secrets.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — long-lived secrets are a standing liability if leaked.

decisions.title: User records are soft-deleted with `deleted_at`; never hard-deleted
decisions.summary: Records are marked deleted with a timestamp instead of being removed.
decisions.context:
- Accidental deletes are recoverable.
- Preserves audit and billing history.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — irreversible and destroys audit and billing history.

decisions.title: Cache LLM responses by normalized prompt hash
decisions.summary: Proposed reusing a cached completion for identical prompts — rejected: stale cache hits masked prompt regressions during iteration.
decisions.context: During local iteration the same prompts run repeatedly. Caching completions by a normalized prompt hash was proposed to cut token spend and make runs reproducible.
decisions.consequences: Caching was dropped. The team keeps calling the model each run and uses a cheaper model for local iteration instead.

alternatives.option: Cache by normalized prompt hash (the proposal)
alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — stale hits hid real changes and made debugging confusing.

decisions.title: Stream tokens to the client over SSE, not WebSocket
decisions.summary: Token streaming uses server-sent events rather than a bidirectional WebSocket.
decisions.context:
- Streaming is one-directional, so SSE is simpler and survives proxies better.
- No need for the overhead of a bidirectional socket.

alternatives.why_rejected: Rejected — bidirectional transport is unnecessary for one-way token streaming.

decision_related.why: billing classifies retryable failures from the gateway error text — it must migrate to the structured status field or it will retry the wrong errors.
the proxy rollout behind INC-3042 broke notifications fan-out — infra and service rollouts must stay decoupled and coordinated.
the gateway authenticates every inbound request against this shared provider.
notifications validates webhook callers through the same provider — changes ripple to delivery.

signals.title: billing still parses error text
signals.text: The new structured `status` field changes the error envelope — billing still parses error text and will break.
signals.consequence: billing classifies success vs. failure by reading the gateway’s error *text*, then feeds that into its retry logic. api-gateway is replacing that text with a structured `status` field. The moment it ships, billing will misread every failure — retrying non-retryable errors and surfacing 500s to customers. This is the exact outage that triggered the change in the first place.
signals.recommended: Migrate billing to read the structured `status` field before api-gateway removes text parsing. Until then, the two are on a collision course.

signals.title: shared OIDC is a single point of dependency
signals.text: Shared OIDC is a single dependency — any change ripples to every service. Broadcast before shipping.
signals.consequence: All six services authenticate against one shared OIDC provider. Any change to it — token format, lifetime, or signing keys — affects every service simultaneously. There is no gradual rollout: a bad change is a group-wide outage, not an isolated one.
signals.recommended: Broadcast any OIDC change to the whole group before shipping, and stage it behind a flag. Treat the provider as a frozen contract unless coordinated.

signals.title: infra rollouts can still alter service behavior
signals.text: Infra deploys are decoupled from service deploys, but proxy rollouts can still alter timeouts. Coordinate rollouts.
signals.consequence: Deploys are decoupled, but infrastructure changes (proxy timeouts, mesh config) still change how running services behave. A proxy rollout already broke notifications-worker once with no code change (INC-3042) — and because the two pipelines look independent, the cause took ~30 minutes to find.
signals.recommended: Announce infra timeout/mesh changes to notifications ahead of time and watch its error rate during rollout.

status-field.description:
The error envelope now carries an explicit `status` field — one of `ok`, `retryable`, or `fatal` — alongside the existing human-readable message. Consumers read **that field**, never the prose.

This keeps three contracts cleanly separated:

- The **HTTP error code** stays pinned for legacy services that cannot change it.
- The **message text** stays free-form, for humans and logs.
- The **`status` field** becomes the single machine-readable signal every consumer agrees on.

## Why the text was never safe
Parsing the message text was never a real contract — it only looked like one. A wording change in the gateway silently flipped `payments-worker` into returning 500s in production, because it matched on specific phrasing. The structured field removes that entire class of failure: copy can change freely without breaking a single downstream consumer.

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: payments-worker is throwing 500s in prod — started ~20 min ago

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: nothing shipped on our side though 🤔

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: it’s reading the gateway error *text* to decide status. we reworded that message this morning.

lines.speaker: Lena Fischer
lines.text: the error *code* is pinned by the legacy contract, so everyone parses the text instead

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: text was never meant to be a contract. that’s the trap

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: fix: add a real `status` field to the error body and have consumers read that, not the text

lines.speaker: Lena Fischer
lines.text: +1 — keep text for humans, structured status for machines

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: on it — api-gateway #482

lines.speaker: PR #482
lines.text: Add structured `status` field to the error envelope; deprecate text parsing. Closes the payments-worker outage from this morning.

lines.speaker: PR #12
lines.text: callers branch on the HTTP status code only; failures are 4xx/5xx

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: how should consumers distinguish success vs failure from the gateway response?

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: the error code is locked — legacy services pinned it in the contract, we can’t change it

lines.speaker: Lena Fischer
lines.text: so read it from the message text? it has the actual reason anyway

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: yeah — parse the text for status for now

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: ok, I’ll document it as the convention

lines.speaker: PR #211
lines.text: Separate the infra deploy pipeline from service deploys after INC-3042. Infra rollouts no longer ride along with application deploys.
proxy v2 rolled out across the platform mesh
notifications-worker error rate climbing — no deploy on their side
on-call investigating; no code changes found in notifications
root cause: the proxy rollout changed default timeouts
resolved — infra rollouts decoupled from service deploys going forward

shared-oidc.description:
All six services delegate authentication to **one shared local OIDC provider** instead of each implementing its own. A request is trusted only if it carries a token that provider issued and signed.

The upside is consistency: one place to reason about identity, one audit surface, and near-zero auth boilerplate when a new service joins the group.

## The dependency this creates
Because everything trusts the same provider, it becomes a **single point of dependency**. Any change to token format, lifetime, or signing keys lands on all six services at once — there is no gradual rollout. Treat the provider as a frozen contract and **broadcast any change to the whole group before shipping it**.

lines.speaker: [00:12] Lena
lines.text: every service rolling its own auth is what’s slowing onboarding down

lines.speaker: [00:31] Marco
lines.text: proposal — one shared local OIDC provider, every service validates against it

lines.speaker: [00:49] Priya
lines.text: that’s a single dependency though — if it changes, everything breaks

lines.speaker: [01:03] Lena
lines.text: right, so any change to it has to be broadcast to the whole group before it ships

lines.speaker: [01:20] Marco
lines.text: agreed — shared OIDC, broadcast-before-change

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: all east-west traffic goes through the gateway — no direct service-to-service calls

lines.speaker: agent
lines.text: use exponential backoff with full jitter, cap at 5 attempts to avoid a thundering herd

lines.speaker: Lena Fischer
lines.text: 15 min access tokens, silent refresh on the client

lines.speaker: PR #88
lines.text: dedupe inbound webhooks by event id; processing must be idempotent

lines.speaker: Sara Okonkwo
lines.text: store money as integer minor units (cents), never floats

lines.speaker: agent
lines.text: evaluate flags per request so rollouts take effect without a redeploy

lines.speaker: PR #150
lines.text: migrations are forward-only in prod; roll forward, never down

lines.speaker: Marco Reyes
lines.text: fan-out via the queue, never synchronous HTTP to N consumers

lines.speaker: agent
lines.text: rate limit by API token, not IP — shared NATs were getting throttled

lines.speaker: Priya Nair
lines.text: store all timestamps as UTC ISO-8601; convert at the edge

lines.speaker: PR #61
lines.text: service-to-service uses short-lived OIDC tokens, not static API keys

lines.speaker: Sara Okonkwo
lines.text: soft-delete user records with deleted_at; never hard-delete

lines.speaker: agent
lines.text: cache completions keyed by a normalized prompt hash to avoid re-calling on identical prompts

lines.speaker: agent
lines.text: use SSE for token streaming — it is one-way anyway and plays nicer with proxies
